labels.title: Which savings or income source will be the largest?
labels.answer: Your largest retirement savings source at age %age% will be your %accountName% and your largest income source will be %incomeSource%.

estatevalue.title: Funds desired at death
estatevalue.definition: Value of your investments, life insurance proceeds, or estate that you wish to leave for your beneficiaries and heirs.

postretirementexpenses.title: Expenses during retirement
postretirementexpenses.definition: Expenses change as you move through various stages of retirement. Three major retirement stages, and the expenses usually associated with each of them, include: 1) Early retirement. Early retirees typically spend more than retirees in their 60s or 70s. Early retirees may travel more often, or even may still be facing mortgage payments. They have yet to face the restricted budgets that affect retirees in later stages of retirement. You are likely to be in a lower income tax bracket since your highest income-earning years have just ended; 2) Middle retirement. As you hit the middle stage of retirement, you will likely begin to spend less, as you travel less, pay off your mortgage, and come to terms with a need to make your retirement fund last. If you are healthy, you are likely to face only some medical bills. Estate planning becomes crucial to ensure that your assets are handed over to the intended beneficiaries when you die; and 3) Late retirement. At this stage, you are spending more on prescription drugs or other medical care. Leisure- and housing-related expenses are at a minimum and you are likely to have completely paid off any debts such as credit cards or auto loans.

regularira.title: Regular IRA
regularira.definition: A regular IRA is also called a traditional IRA. It is a tax-deferred retirement account. For 2025, you are allowed to make a tax-deferred contribution of $7,000 to a regular IRA. This account grows tax-deferred until you begin to take distributions, which you may do without penalty after you turn age 59-1/2. For persons who are age 50 or older, a special catch-up provision of the 2001 tax law allows you to contribute an additional $1,000, or a total of $8,000.

rothira.title: Roth IRA
rothira.definition: A tax-advantaged retirement account that allows you to make an after-tax contribution of up to $7,000 for 2025, or up to $8,000 if you're age 50 or older. If you keep a Roth IRA for at least five years and are at least age 59-1/2 when you begin to withdraw from the account, the entire account may be distributed tax- and penalty-free.

dcplan401k.title: 401(k) plan
dcplan401k.definition: A 401(k) plan is a defined-contribution plan that permits employees to have a portion of their salary deducted from their paycheck and contributed to an account. Federal (and usually state) taxes on the employee contributions and investment earnings are deferred until the participant receives a distribution from the plan (typically at retirement). Employers may also contribute to the account of a participant. For 2025, you may contribute up to $23,500 to a 401(k) plan. If you are age 50 or older, you may contribute an additional $7,500, or a total of $31,000. If your employer makes contributions, the most that may be contributed to a 401(k) plan for 2025 is the lesser of $70,000 ($76,500 if age 50 or older) or 100% of your annual compensation.

increaseddcplancontributions.title: Increased contributions to retirement plans
increaseddcplancontributions.definition: The Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2001 authorizes higher yearly contribution limits to IRAs, 401(k) plans, and other defined-contribution retirement plans. For 401(k) plans, the yearly limit is $23,500 in 2025. A catch-up provision of the law authorizes even higher limits for workers who are age 50 or older. For these employees, the yearly limit is $30,500 in 2025.

catchupprovisiondcplans.title: Catch-up provision for contributions to retirement plans
catchupprovisiondcplans.definition: A provision of The Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2001 that authorizes higher yearly contribution limits to IRAs, 401(k) plans, and other defined-contribution retirement plans for workers who are age 50 or older.

inflation.title: Inflation
inflation.definition: A general increase in prices that you pay for goods and services, stated as a yearly rate.

taxableaccount.title: Taxable account
taxableaccount.definition: An account that does not receive the tax breaks that either a tax-exempt account or tax-deferred account are eligible to receive.

rateofreturn.title: Rate of return
rateofreturn.definition: The percentage gain or loss on an investment expressed as a yearly rate.

pension.title: Pension
pension.definition: An employer-sponsored retirement plan that pays retirees a fixed amount that is based on number of years of service and salary history.

increasediracontributions.title: Increased contributions to individual retirement accounts
increasediracontributions.definition: The Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2001 authorizes higher yearly contribution limits to IRAs, 401(k) plans, and other defined-contribution retirement plans. For regular and Roth IRAs, the yearly limit is $7,000 in 2025. A catch-up provision of the new law authorizes even higher limits for those who are age 50 or older. For these investors, the yearly combined limit is $8,000 in 2025. Employees participating in a 401(k) plan can now open a regular 401(k) or a Roth 401(k) within the plan account.

roth401k.title: Qualified Roth contribution programs
roth401k.definition: A tax-advantaged retirement account similar to a Roth IRA with one exception: there are no income limitations for those who want to participate.

taxadvantagedaccount.title: Tax-advantaged account
taxadvantagedaccount.definition: An investment account with tax-deferred or tax-exempt features that are used to save for retirement, college, and other educational expenses.

catchupprovisioniras.title: Catch-up provision for contributions to individual retirement accounts
catchupprovisioniras.definition: The Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2001 authorized higher yearly contribution limits to IRAs, 401(k) plans and other defined-contribution retirement plans. A catch-up provision of the new law authorized even higher limits for workers who are age 50 or older. For regular and Roth IRAs, this additional contribution amount is $1,000 for 2025. As a result, combined yearly contribution limits for persons aged 50 or older increase to $8,000 for 2025.

help.textLabel: This tool shows the breakdown of savings accumulated by account at retirement as well as income by source in the first year of retirement.
Here are some tips to help you ensure that estimated expenses during retirement will be covered:
Save early, regularly, and stick to the plan. The sooner you start saving, the more time your money has to grow.
Keep working. Retire later to give your portfolio the chance to continue increasing in value for a few more years.
Put money into tax-advantaged accounts. Good options include traditional IRAs, Roth IRAs, Roth 401(k)s, and 401(k)s or similar employer-sponsored retirement plans.
Diversify your investment portfolio. The more diverse a portfolio, the safer it is.
Pay down higher interest debt like credit cards to free up more money for retirement plan contributions
Purchase long-term care insurance to reduce medical expenses in retirement
In addition, this tool factors in maximum contribution limits to retirement accounts based on the tax laws set by the IRS.
